perturbation.original_text: Any software and documentation provided under this Agreement is provided with RESTRICTED RIGHTS. Use, duplication, or disclosure by the Government is subject to restrictions as set forth in subparagraph (c)(1)(ii) of The Rights in Technical Data and Computer Software clause at DFARS 252.227-7013 or subparagraphs (c)(1) and (2) of the Commercial Computer Software -- Restricted Rights at 48 CFR 52.227-19, as applicable. Manufacturer is Microsoft Corporation/One Microsoft Way/Redmond, WA 98052-6399.
perturbation.changed_text: Manufacturer is Microsoft Corporation/One Microsoft Way/Redmond, WA 98052-6399. Any software and documentation provided under this Agreement is provided with RESTRICTED RIGHTS. Use, duplication, or disclosure by the Government is subject to restrictions as set forth in subparagraph (c)(1)(ii) of The Rights in Technical Data and Computer Software clause at DFARS 252.227-7013 or subparagraphs (c)(1) and (2) of the Commercial Computer Software -- Restricted Rights at 48 CFR 52.227-19, as applicable.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the manufacturer's address at the beginning, the crucial RESTRICTED RIGHTS clause is de-emphasized and made less noticeable, especially to government entities that need to understand their usage restrictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: This relocation buries the critical 'Restricted Rights' clause, which is mandated by 48 CFR § 52.227-14 for government contracts involving software. By obscuring the clause and placing the manufacturer information first, the government's rights concerning the software's use, duplication, and disclosure are less prominent, making it harder for government users to be aware of and comply with the restrictions, and thus contradicts the explicit requirements for clear communication of these rights.

perturbation.justification: The modified text rearranges the original text such that the 'RESTRICTED RIGHTS' clause is de-emphasized by placing the manufacturer's address at the beginning. The law explanation states that this relocation buries the critical 'Restricted Rights' clause, which is mandated by 48 CFR § 52.227-14. The Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction states that a legal contradiction arises when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is directly related to 48 CFR 52.227-14, indicating this code is relevant. This supports the assessment that the altered structure creates a legal contradiction by undermining the prominence and accessibility of the legally required 'Restricted Rights' clause, thus hindering compliance and awareness.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive or equitable relief as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive or', this can lead the receiving party to believe that monetary damages are waived, which is often not the case and is a legal right of the disclosing party.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting 'without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive or', this change implies the disclosing party is limited only to equitable relief and waives rights to other remedies like monetary damages in Washington. This contradicts RCW 62A.2-703, which grants the seller (Disclosing Party) multiple remedies for breach of contract by the buyer (Receiving Party), including damages, and does not force an election of remedies unless explicitly stated. The omission suggests a limitation that doesn't inherently exist under Washington law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 62A.2-703: Seller's remedies in general. Print RCWs > Title 62A > Chapter 62A.2 > Section 62A.2-703 62A.2-702 << 62A.2-703 >> 62A.2-704 PDF RCW 62A.2-703 Seller's remedies in general. Where the buyer wrongfully rejects or revokes acceptance of goods or fails to make a payment due on or before delivery or repudiates with respect to a part or the whole, then with respect to any goods directly affected and, if the breach is of the whole contract (RCW 62A.2-612 ), then also with respect to the whole undelivered balance, the aggrieved seller may (a) withhold delivery of such goods; (b) stop delivery by any bailee as hereafter provided (RCW 62A.2-705 ); (c) proceed under the next section respecting goods still unidentified to the contract; (d) resell and recover damages as hereafter provided (RCW 62A.2-706 ); (e) recover damages for non-acceptance (RCW 62A.2-708 ) or in a proper case the price (RCW 62A.2-709 ); (f) cancel. [1965 ex.s. c 157 s 2-703. Cf. former RCW sections: (i) RCW 63.04.540 ; 1925 ex.s. c 142 s 53 ; RRS s 5836-53. (ii) RCW 63.04.620 (1); 1925 ex.s. c 142 s 61 ; RRS s 5836-61.]
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the phrase 'without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive or', potentially leading to the interpretation that the disclosing party is limited to equitable relief and waives other remedies. The Law Explanation supports this interpretation, stating that this contradicts RCW 62A.2-703, which grants the seller (Disclosing Party) multiple remedies. Scraped Law Snippet 1, RCW 62A.2-703, confirms that the seller has multiple remedies, including recovering damages. The changed text creates a contradiction by potentially limiting the seller's remedies when the law provides for multiple options.
